coredata.dc:title: Experimental and numerical electro-thermal analysis of a pouch cell battery accounting for connection bar effects via equivalent boundary conditions on tabs

coredata.dc:description: The performance of lithium-ion batteries is strongly affected by temperature. This study presents a coupled electro-thermal model of a lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxides pouch cell battery (Li-NMC), via a 2D and 3D approach for both electrical and thermal problems, respectively, validated through an experimental analysis. In particular, the model accounts for dispersive bars, which cause heat losses in both experiments and conventional use. Different fitting equations have been formulated for closure parameters, included a piecewise fitting for entropy coefficient. Convection was modelled using heat transfer correlations, providing a reproducible model with all parameters explicitly reported. Three configurations were analyzed: (i) excluding the connection bars, (ii) including them, and (iii) considering their thermal effects via a lumped capacitance method, by using an equivalent boundary condition on the battery tabs. An experimental validation was performed via thermal imaging. The model excluding bars overestimated the maximum temperature by 8.08 °C root mean square error (RMSE) for a 5C discharge, whereas the model including bars reduced this to 2.16 °C. Lumped capacitance model accurately reproduced average and maximum temperature trends, obtaining a maximum difference between the models of 0.18 °C for 5C discharge on the average temperature. The methodology was extended to a multi-cell battery module. Both maximum and average temperatures were very similar when replacing the busbar domain with equivalent boundary conditions, with deviations lower than 0.85 °C. This confirms the validity of the proposed approach in reducing computational effort while maintaining predictive accuracy at both cell and module level.

head.ce:title: Experimental and numerical electro-thermal analysis of a pouch cell battery accounting for connection bar effects via equivalent boundary conditions on tabs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The performance of lithium-ion batteries is strongly affected by temperature. This study presents a coupled electro-thermal model of a lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxides pouch cell battery (Li-NMC), via a 2D and 3D approach for both electrical and thermal problems, respectively, validated through an experimental analysis. In particular, the model accounts for dispersive bars, which cause heat losses in both experiments and conventional use. Different fitting equations have been formulated for closure parameters, included a piecewise fitting for entropy coefficient. Convection was modelled using heat transfer correlations, providing a reproducible model with all parameters explicitly reported. Three configurations were analyzed: (i) excluding the connection bars, (ii) including them, and (iii) considering their thermal effects via a lumped capacitance method, by using an equivalent boundary condition on the battery tabs. An experimental validation was performed via thermal imaging. The model excluding bars overestimated the maximum temperature by 8.08 °C root mean square error (RMSE) for a 5C discharge, whereas the model including bars reduced this to 2.16 °C. Lumped capacitance model accurately reproduced average and maximum temperature trends, obtaining a maximum difference between the models of 0.18 °C for 5C discharge on the average temperature. The methodology was extended to a multi-cell battery module. Both maximum and average temperatures were very similar when replacing the busbar domain with equivalent boundary conditions, with deviations lower than 0.85 °C. This confirms the validity of the proposed approach in reducing computational effort while maintaining predictive accuracy at both cell and module level.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing demand for energy storage systems in electric vehicles, renewable energy grids, and portable electronics, has brought to an increased use of batteries [1]. The global demand for batteries continues to grow and is projected to increase by 25 % annually, reaching an energy demand of 2600 GWh by 2030, with the main drivers of this increase in demand are the electrification of transportation and the implementation of batteries in electricity grids [2]. Lithium-ion batteries are the most widely used among all battery chemistries due to their high energy density [3]. This is particularly important for automotive applications, as it enables longer vehicle range with reduced battery pack weight [4]. In addition, they can support voltages above 3.6 V, offer fast recharging, a longer cycle life, and the ability to achieve high peak load currents. The designing and production of these batteries in Europe is growing rapidly, driven by directives aimed at reducing air pollution [5].

Li-ion batteries operate under conditions where heat generation is inevitable due to internal resistances and electrochemical reactions. If not properly managed, excessive heat accumulation can lead to uneven temperature distributions, reduced performance, accelerated aging [6], and, in extreme cases, thermal runaway [7] and safety hazards. To avoid these problems, a comprehensive understanding of heat generation mechanisms and efficient heat dissipation strategies are required to ensure reliability, efficiency, and safety in battery operation. Knowing the battery temperature is also crucial because their electrical properties, such as open-circuit potential [8], and the internal resistance [9], are temperature-dependent. Consequently, analyzing battery operation also requires a precise understanding of its temperature. For this reason, different coupled thermal models are explored, where battery heat generations from electrochemical reactions are estimated through either electrochemical-thermal models or electro-thermal models.

Battery heat generation arises from chemical processes and internal resistances, requiring coupled thermal, electrical, and chemical descriptions. The heat generation can be categorized into two types: irreversible heating, arising from the battery's resistive properties, and reversible heat generation, driven by chemical reactions within the battery that cause entropy changes in the system. Reversible heat generation, which can be either positive or negative, is mathematically described by Bernardi's equation [10].

Coupled thermal models of batteries can be classified into two main categories: electrochemical-thermal models and electro-thermal models. The former calculates battery heat generation using lithium-ion diffusion equations to simulate the battery's chemistry, while the latter evaluates heat generation based on electrical parameters such as internal resistance and open-circuit voltage. These models are interdependent because the parameters of the electrical or electrochemical models are needed to determine heat generation in the thermal model, and themselves are temperature-dependent, adding non-linearity to the problem.

Electrochemical-thermal models offer a comprehensive description of battery chemical behavior; however, their complexity needs of increased computational demand. One of the most adopted models is the pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) model, introduced by T. F. Fuller et al. [11,12]. Different simplifications of the model have been proposed to reduce its complexity, as summarized in [13].

With electro-thermal models one computes heat generation based on electrical components to construct circuit networks. The Rint model is the simplest one, because it represents the battery as an ideal voltage source in series with its internal resistance, as modelled in [14]. Thevenin models are thermal networks composed of resistors and capacitors arranged in different orders. With each additional order, a resistor is added in parallel with a capacitor. These networks are typically represented as first- or second-order systems. In [15], an enhanced first-order Thevenin model is proposed, incorporating the influence of temperature on the calculation of the open-circuit potential. The Newman, Tiedemann, Gu, and Kim (NTGK) model [16] is also widely used, as it accounts for electrode polarization by introducing semi-empirical parameters, thereby improving the formulation of the voltage – current relationship.

Today, achieving a balance between model complexity and accuracy is critical for many applications, enabling integration into microprocessors using reduced-order modeling while providing precise real-time results [17]. In this context, electro-thermal models are more suitable than electrochemical-thermal models, since the former provide reliable results with lower computational effort. In [18], a method to estimate battery core and surface temperatures using a thermal model coupled with impedance measurements and Kalman filter techniques, is proposed. In [19], a model within SPICE-compatible software, calculates the battery temperature, capturing electro-thermal behavior and demonstrating its utility in practical battery circuit simulations.

Models are also crucial for various thermal management analyses. For example, in [20], the NTGK model was applied to study the thermal management of a battery with a finned surface, revealing a temperature difference of up to 8.6 °C compared to a flat surface.

Experimental analyses provide crucial data for developing and validating electro-thermal models of batteries. Different studies have been conducted to understand the temperature behavior with different applied currents [21], or for different batteries and different fluids as in [22]. Based on experimental data, various validations have been made. For example, different electro-thermal models, including a uniform heat generation for a pouch cell battery, are analyzed, comparing the temperature results of the models with the experimental ones in [23].

Particular attention has also been paid to how cells interact with external components, such as connecting bars in experimental setups or busbars in battery packs. These conductive components serve as both electrical pathways and thermal bridges, significantly influencing the temperature distribution. In experimental setups, some models apply an increased convective heat transfer coefficient to the battery tabs compared to the surface [23], accounting for the heat flux between the tabs and the bars. Regarding busbar connections in battery packs, [24] compared a physical busbar connection with a virtual one under real operating currents, finding temperature differences of up to 28.43 °C. In [25], the behavior of battery interconnections was studied, highlighting the importance of studying their topology in thermal management design. Innovative busbar designs have also been proposed; for instance, [26] investigated a busbar acting as a turbulator for an 18650 battery, achieving a maximum temperature reduction of up to 3.81 %.

As already mentioned, batteries in practical applications are always interconnected, making the bars an important factor in their electro-thermal behavior. Neglecting their contribution could lead to inaccurate model predictions and weaken the basis for experimental validation. In addition, despite the growing literature on electro-thermal modeling of lithium-ion pouch cells, many studies remain incomplete, often neglecting key parameters such as internal resistances, entropic contributions, or thermal properties, which limits reproducibility and rigorous validation. Moreover, the treatment of heat transfer with the environment is frequently modelled by assuming a fixed convective heat transfer coefficient [27,28]. However, more accurate estimates can be obtained through empirical correlations.

In contrast, the present study provides all the necessary data and trends to enable full validation and to ensure the reliability of the proposed model. Furthermore, to the best of our knowledge, no previous work has presented a comprehensive approach that combines: (i) a complete electro-thermal model; (ii) the use of empirical correlations to properly account for natural convection; and (iii) an analytical treatment of the influence of the connection bars, starting from the full battery architecture down to the elementary cell electrical model, via an equivalent and fast predictive model to account for this dispersion effect. In addition, this study offers a detailed and rigorous derivation of the pouch cell model, starting from the full battery architecture down to the elementary cell electrical model. Each step of the modeling process — both thermal and electrical — is explicitly justified, equation by equation, with careful consideration of the physical significance and specific characteristics of pouch cells. Moreover, all simplifications introduced in the model are clearly motivated and supported by physical reasoning, ensuring that they do not compromise accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency. This thorough presentation ensures transparency and reproducibility, providing a clear framework that can be reliably applied and extended in future research and practical engineering applications.

Special attention is given to the role of connecting bars in the experimental setup whose influence is analyzed through detailed finite element simulations and, in reduced form, via a lumped capacitance approach using an equivalent boundary condition applied to the battery tabs, justified by a Biot number Bi for the tabs much smaller than 0.1, as discussed in Section 2.3. This approach allows to capture their thermal effect while reducing computational cost.

The methodology is validated against in-house experimental data, demonstrating that neglecting the bars leads to discrepancies in predicted cell temperatures, while their inclusion significantly improves agreement in terms of both maximum and average values. The proposed methodology will then simplify the experimental analysis model, where the omission of connecting bars can simplify the geometry, and lower model complexity. The analysis is then extended to a battery module, where the cells are interconnected through busbars. In this case as well, the busbars are modelled using the equivalent boundary condition on tabs, demonstrating that the approach remains valid at module level and can be reliably extended to the battery pack scale, while further reducing computational requirements.

The pouch cell consists of multiple layers of active and conductive materials that facilitate the conversion of chemical energy into electrical energy through redox reactions. The internal structure of a pouch cell is arranged in layers and includes the following components:
•
Electrodes (positive and negative): Made from an active and porous material, they store and release lithium ions during discharge and charge.
•
Separator: Layer of porous material, that allows lithium-ion to pass between cathode and anode while preventing direct contact, thereby reducing the risk of short circuits.
•
Current collectors (positive and negative): Electrically conductive layers that collect electrons from or deliver them to the active materials in the electrodes, allowing the electrons to flow through the external circuit.

The structure is periodic, as shown in Fig. 1 , with the electrodes arranged in an alternating “sandwich” configuration alongside the current collectors. The elementary cell (EC) is intended as a representative layer to characterize the electro-chemical and thermal phenomena, that occurs inside the battery. It consists of two layers of each component, except for the positive current collector (only one component) and the negative current collector (two halves of two components). Multiple elementary cells are stacked together to form the battery core, with each cell electrically connected in parallel to the others. In addition to the elementary cells arranged in a row, the battery core is composed of an extra half of a negative current collector, an extra negative electrode, and an extra separator layer for each side. This means that there are additional layers on the negative side compared to the positive side. This arrangement helps to ensure a more uniform current and potential distribution across the cell, enhancing electrical stability. In summary, the number of each cell component in the battery core as a function of the number of elementary cells N
EC is shown in Table 1 .

A coupled electro-thermal model for a pouch cell battery has been developed here to account for the interdependent nature of thermal and electrical behaviours within the cell. This coupling is essential, as the heat generation in the battery is dependent from the elementary cell resistance
R EC
, that in turn varies with temperature T too. Consequently, the thermal model relies on electrical characteristics, while the electrical model, in turn, is influenced by temperature T , introducing non-linearity into the system, and creating a reciprocal dependency that accurately reflects the battery's behavior under operational conditions.

The two-dimensional electrical model is referred to a single elementary cell, and is two-dimensional, representing only the surfaces of the current collectors. The influence of the other battery components is modelled through a modified first-order Thevenin model, thus avoiding their direct representation. In the 2D domain, only the current collectors are analyzed because the in-plane current flow is primarily confined to these layers due to their significantly higher electrical conductivity σ
compared to the other cell components [23]. This two-dimensional characterization is crucial for accurately capturing local hotspots that may arise within pouch cells, which are caused by non-uniform heat generation resulting from spatial variation in current across the surfaces of the current collectors. Indeed, even if small, there are huge effects on the temperature distribution along the plane [23,27]. This localized heat generation can lead to the formation of hotspots, which may irreversibly damage the cell, causing malfunctions or speeding up the aging problems. Moreover, hotspots can trigger the thermal runaway phenomenon by initiating the decomposition of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) at elevated temperatures — around 90 °C — which initiates a cascade of reactions that generate progressively higher heat generations, further accelerating the process. A three-dimensional model for the current collectors is excluded, as their thickness is negligible compared to their width and length and the electric potential φ distribution along the thickness for this reason is considered constant. The elementary cell was simplified using symmetry properties, by joining the two negative current collectors' halves, the electrodes, and the separators, as in Fig. 2 .

Lithium ions move from one current collector to another depending on charge or discharge. The flow of electric current through the thickness of an elementary cell
I EC
can be summarized by an equivalent circuit, with an elementary cell resistance
R EC
. This parameter is derived from the total battery resistance
R B
, calculated through a modified first-order Thevenin electrical circuit model [29,30], with two resistors in series with an RC loop, as shown in Fig. 3 . It is referred to as ‘modified’ because, although it is theoretically a double RC loop, the capacitance of the first loop is considered negligible and is therefore synthesized into a single equivalent resistor. The circuit parameters consist of:
•
Elementary cell current
I EC
: Electric current circulating in the elementary cell, the direction depends on whether it is charging or discharging.
•
Open-circuit voltage
V OC
: Voltage measured across its terminals when no external load is connected.
•
Internal resistance
R I
: Ohmic resistance due to its internal components, such as the electrodes, separator, and current collectors.
•
Activation polarization resistance
R
P , ACT
: Arises from the activation energy needed to drive electro-chemical reactions at the electrode surfaces.
•
Concentration polarization resistance
R
P , CON
: Due to concentration gradients that develop when ions are unable to move fast enough to sustain the reaction rate at the electrodes.
•
Concentration polarization capacity
C
P , CON
: Refers to the ability of the system to temporarily store charge in the form of concentration gradients, which arise when ionic transport is insufficient to sustain the reaction rate at the electrodes. This value is assessed using a characteristic time constant
τ
P , CON
, given the challenges in precisely characterizing the battery's capacitive behavior through experimental analyses.
•
Battery potential difference
ΔV
: Voltage measured across its terminals when an external load is connected.

R B

I EC

I B
, because all the elementary cells are electrically connected in parallel between them. The total battery resistance
R B
can be described by an exponential function to depict the capacitive behavior, only when a step current is applied and if the battery current
I B
remains constant thereafter.

The open-circuit voltage, as well as the resistances and capacity of the electrical circuit model, are determined through experimental analyses. The temperature T results will be shown as a function of the depth of discharge (DoD) which is defined as the fraction of the battery's total capacity C that has been discharged. It is directly related to the state of charge, through the relationship DoD = 1 − SoC .

To set this initial condition, it is assumed that prior to

During the relaxation phase, the internal electrochemical processes of the battery, including ions redistribution and the re-establishment of charge equilibrium, act to restore the battery to a stable state. As a result, the battery's open circuit voltage
V OC

I B

As with the Dirichlet boundary condition applied to the negative tab, a zero-potential φ condition is used as the reference point for the system's electric potential.

The three-dimensional thermal model is applied to the whole battery and to the bars. The model is three-dimensional because the equivalent thermal conductivity k in the z-direction, calculated as a series of thermal conductivities along the thickness, is the lowest among all directions. Consequently, there are significant temperature gradients along the z-direction, as also demonstrated by [31]. The thermal influence of the bars is significant due to their high thermal conductivity k . Since they are typically made of copper, they provide a low thermal resistance, creating a preferential path for heat transfer compared to convective heat transfer. Additionally, the bars generate heat through Joule heating as a result of the current flowing through them. The necessity of including the connection bars in the computational domain will be demonstrated in the Results and discussion section, where the outcomes of simulations with and without bars will be compared against experimental data for two constant current discharge rates of 3C and 5C. Where C is the C-rate, which indicates how fast the battery is charged or discharged relative to its capacity. For example, a C-rate of 1C means the battery would fully charge or discharge in 1 h; 3C means it would take about 20 min, and 5C about 12 min.

The entire battery in the thermal model will consist of the battery core, the tabs, and an external case. Since many components are involved, the battery core will be a single equivalent component with density ρ and specific heat c , calculated through volume and mass weighted average, respectively, across the thicknesses of all the layers of which it is composed, under the assumption that each core component shares the same surface area, as detailed in Eqs. (12), (13). The equivalent thermal conductivity k is orthotropic and is derived through the theory of equivalent thermal networks. Specifically, the equivalent thermal conductivity k is calculated in series along the z-direction and in parallel along the x- and y-directions, Eqs. (14), (15). The reason for modeling the core in this way is to reduce the complexity of the computational domain, while obtaining a component with equivalent thermal properties.

N EC
. This thickness ratio, always smaller than 1, is applied because the core is represented as a single homogeneous component, even though this heat generation occurs exclusively within the current collectors. In this way, the heat dissipation is effectively redistributed as a uniform generation across the core, while reflecting, with a good approximation, that the contribution originates solely from the current collectors' thickness.

Reversible heat generation
Q ̇
rev
: Originates from the entropy changes during the electro-chemical reactions. It is an equivalent parameter that encapsulates the thermal contributions of the various chemical reactions that occur within the battery. The reversible heat generation in a battery is evaluated using Bernardi's equation [10], and is directly proportional to the current collector current
I cc
, the number of elementary cells
N EC
, the temperature T , and the entropy coefficient ∂
V OC
/ ∂ T . The entropy coefficient, which depends on the state of charge, can be either positive or negative, reflecting the variability in entropy changes during operation. This parameter describes the variation of open-circuit voltage
V OC
with temperature T due to entropy changes at a given SoC. While these variations in
V OC
are negligible, the entropy coefficient plays a significant role in heat generation, as it determines whether the process is exothermic or endothermic during charging and discharging. This variability highlights that heat generation depends strongly on the entropy changes associated with different states of charge.

Initial and boundary conditions for battery case, bars and tabs are reported in the following and resumed in Fig. 5. First, within the boundaries that link the core with the tabs, and tabs and bars, temperature T continuity conditions are invoked for all the variables involved. For the current equation, it is assumed that current is constant and uniform across the bars, with the same values obtained at the boundaries of the tab's domain. The thermal boundary condition of the tabs is incorporated into that of the batteries, as the tabs are approximated as a vertical flat plate with a length
L B
equivalent to the battery's case height, assuming that the tab height is negligible compared to the battery case one. Therefore, any reference to the thermal boundary conditions of the battery case inherently includes the tabs.

Given the interdependence of temperature and electrical parameters, say the coupling of the electrical model with temperature and the thermal model with electrical parameters, the two models must be mutually integrated [23]. The thermal model retrieves all the necessary electrical parameters from the electrical model to accurately calculate heat generation across all components, including the current collector current
I cc
, the elementary cell resistance
R EC
, and the potential gradients ∇ φ in the current collectors. Conversely, the electrical model receives a two-dimensional temperature field
T
2 D
, from the thermal model. This temperature field is obtained by integral averaging along the core thickness
d co
, as in Eq. (27), resulting in a two-dimensional representation where battery resistance
R B
is calculated as a function of this temperature field. The integral average is performed exclusively over the core domain since the battery resistance
R B
is effective only within this region. A schematization of the way of coupling is depicted in Fig. 6 .

Finally, it should be noted that the sensitivity of the conclusions to the cell capacity C was not explicitly investigated in this study, since the primary objective was to evaluate the thermal influence of conductive interconnections. Similar analyses focusing on the role of cell capacity can be found in [35].

To exclude from the computational domain the bars connected to the battery, a lumped capacitance method can be employed for them. In this case, there is no required finite element analysis modeling for that part of the domain, that is often included in experimental runs and also in practice in battery packs through the use of busbars. It is also remarked that faster models are mandatory in this field because of the real-time monitoring of batteries health. This approach can be utilized if the Biot number Bi of the bars is lower than 0.10 [36] and it is given by the equation below.
(28)

where

Λ bar
, to its convective heat transfer area with the air,
A bar
. At such low Bi values, the convective thermal resistance between the bar and the ambient is significantly greater than the conductive thermal resistance within the bar. Consequently, the temperature distribution T within the bar is nearly uniform, justifying its approximation as a single point while neglecting its spatial dimensions. In the analyzed cases, the study is conducted under natural convection with air, which is associated with relatively low convective heat transfer coefficients h . However, the same approach is expected to remain valid also under forced convection, since the characteristic lengths
L c
of the bars and their high thermal conductivity k allow for much higher values of the convective heat transfer coefficient. In the present analysis with experimental bars, values up to approximately h ≈ 238
W /
m 2

L c
is considered, equal to the higher bar dimension, i.e. the bar length

The battery used in this study is an EIG ePLB C020, a lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (Li-NMC) pouch cell with a nominal capacity of 20 Ah, comprising 17 elementary cells
N EC
[37]. Detailed specifications of this battery are provided in Table 2 , taken from [38]. In Fig. 9 are presented both an image of the battery (a) and a schematic representation (b). The measured dimensions, along with the thicknesses, and the properties of each core component and of the case (taken from [37], with the electrical conductivity σ from [39]), are summarized in in Tables 3 and 4 , respectively.

A total of nine experimental data points were collected, corresponding to the state of charge range of 0.1 to 0.9, with one data point acquired for each 0.1 increment in SoC. To extend the analysis across the full SoC range, two quadratic extrapolations were performed to estimate the values at SoC = 0 and SoC = 1 . For SoC = 0 , the extrapolation is obtained based on the experimental points between 0 ≤ SoC ≤ 0.3 , while for SoC = 1 , between 0.7 ≤ SoC ≤ 1 . The SoC ranges used for extrapolation are intentionally limited to ensure accuracy and consistency. This decision arises from the observation that experimental data points located near the boundaries of the selected range exhibit significant variability compared to those within the range. For this reason, the entropy coefficient ∂
V OC
/ ∂ T values in the SoC range between 0.3 and 0.7 are not considered for the extrapolation. Such variations indicate a different behavior in the data, which, if included, could lead to inaccurate estimations. Finally, a case study with five batteries connected in series is analyzed, representing either a possible battery module or a row of pouch cells within a battery pack. The spacing between the cells
d B
is set to 8 mm, under natural convection conditions. This choice aims to maximize the convective heat flux: if the cells are too close, viscous forces reduce the airflow between them, lowering the convective heat transfer; on the other hand, if the spacing is too large, the thermal boundary layers do not interact, again reducing the convective transfer. For this reason, an optimized spacing can be found in the literature, as proposed by [41], by modeling the batteries as parallel plates with the characteristic length
L B
taken as the total cell height, excluding the tabs. In this case as well, all thermal properties of air are evaluated at the film temperature.

In this case, instead of the bars used in the experimental setup, busbars made of copper are considered, with a width
W bb
equal to that of the tabs, a length
L bb
sufficient to connect the cells in series, and a thickness
d bb
of 0.2 mm. The busbars connecting multiple batteries act as isothermal bridges, equalizing the temperatures of the tabs they connect. For the thermal boundary condition of the busbars, the same correlations as those used for the connecting bars are applied. In addition, a symmetry condition is imposed on the busbars located at the outer sides of the batteries, simulating a parallel connection with neighboring cells. The case without busbars is also analyzed. In this configuration, the boundary condition at the tab is still derived from the balance reported in Eq. (29). Fig. 11 shows the computational domain with and without busbars. The final objective is to compare the temperature fields and maximum and average temperatures of the two configurations, highlighting that neglecting the busbars in the computational domain leads to an error that can be considered negligible with respect to the standard configuration.

The aim of this study is to thermally characterize a pouch cell battery accounting also for the different currents that are flowing in the analyzed domain. To achieve this, the experimental investigation and validation focuses on analyzing the surface temperature fields T , average temperature
T avg
, and maximum temperature
T max
of the battery. After completing the “formation process”, a step designed to create a uniform and stable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer on the cell anode [42], two constant-current discharge rates were examined, corresponding to complete discharges at 3C and 5C. These rates were selected as limit values, because they represent the recommended discharge current (3C) and the maximum continuous discharge current (5C), respectively, as indicated in Table 2. The battery cells were positioned vertically and subjected to air natural convection, at a temperature
T amb
of 25 °C.

The discharge protocols were programmed using two Eltra cyclers: the E-8094 model, operating with a nominal voltage range of 3.6 to 6.0 V and a current range of 0 to 280 A, and the portable E-8325 model, supporting voltages from 0 to 18 V, with a maximum charging current of 80 A and a maximum discharging current of 150 A. The temperature distribution T across the surface was monitored using a Flir ThermaCAM S40 infrared camera, while data analysis was conducted using ThermaCAM Researcher Pro 2.7 software, capable of calculating the average and maximum surface temperatures
T avg
,
T max
from thermal images, only on the surface encompassing the batteries, the tabs, and the portion of the bars up to a fixed distance from the outermost side of the battery,
D D
= 16
mm . The camera properties are listed in Table 6 . Thermal imaging during transient conditions was performed at fixed intervals, ranging from seconds to several minutes depending on the rate of temperature change ∂ T / ∂ t . Temperature measurements were carried out exclusively with the infrared camera, since this method provides a non-intrusive and spatially resolved temperature field, which is particularly suitable for pouch cells. Unlike thermocouples, which can introduce local perturbations and provide only point-wise data, infrared thermography allows capturing both the maximum and average surface temperatures, which are the key validation quantities for the present model. The measurement accuracy of the thermal camera was equal to ±2 °C or ±2 % of the measured value, whichever is greater, based on the manufacturer specifications, as specified in Table 6.

The battery, previously shown in Fig. 9, is secured with copper bars with a symmetric ‘L’-shaped cross-section, with its dimensions shown in Fig. 12 , where on the left the cross-sectional area is shown, using a symmetry axis along the diagonal with a chain thin line, and on the right the upper view is depicted, showing its length. The value of the cross-sectional area for current flow in the bars, which is also essential for determining Joule heat generation
Q ̇
i , j , jou
, using Eq. (43).
(43)

In Fig. 13(a) the battery with mounted bars, used during the discharge process for thermal imaging, is illustrated. Screws were employed to fasten each bar to the corresponding tab, while tabs were bent to ensure a secure connection. To simplify the computational domain, screws are excluded as their size is negligible relative to the system, their thermal conductivity k are similar to to the bar one
k bar
, and their thermal contact resistance is deemed marginal. In addition, tab bending is not considered in the model, as the tabs maintain an almost constant temperature during operation due to the reduced conductive thermal resistance inside them. This behavior, coupled with their relatively simple geometry, does not result in significant differences in thermal simulations. Since the temperature variation along the tab is negligible compared to other thermal phenomena (such as thermal interaction with the cells and the bars), its impact on the overall energy balance of the system is minimal. A schematic representation of the computational domain of the battery with the bars is shown in Fig. 13(b), highlighting key dimensions such as
D D
, representing the distance from the outermost side of the battery to the corresponding portion of the bar considered in the thermal imaging,
D E
, which corresponds to the relative position of the bar with compared to the negative tab, and
D F
, equal to the relative position of the bar compared to the tab in the y-z plane (this applies to both tabs). In the x-y plane, the edge of the positive tab aligns with the edge of the bar, in contrast to the negative tab. In the y-z plane, the tab is modelled at the center relative to its bar width
W
bar , j
. However, as discussed in Section 2.3, these details do not affect the results in the lumped capacitance method model, as the relative positioning of the bars with respect to the tabs does not influence the results.

The complete discharge during the experimental campaign was stopped before reaching a state of charge equal to zero for a 5C discharge, arriving at a DoD = 57 % because the battery temperature T was approaching the operating temperature limit of 50 °C, as shown in Table 2. At high discharge rates like 5C discharge, a significant Joule heat generation
Q ̇
jou
has occurred, because it is proportional to the square of the elementary cell current
I EC
2
, which increases with the discharge rate applied. If the temperature rises above 50 °C, it can cause irreversible damage to the battery's chemistry [43,44], reducing its capacity C and potentially compromising safety, favoring the thermal runaway phenomenon. For a 3C discharge the experimental analysis started from a full charge to a SoC = 6 % .

It should be noted that the L-shaped connecting bars employed in the experimental setup are determined by the laboratory apparatus and do not reflect an optimized design for battery modules. Their role in this study is not to represent the actual geometry of module busbars, but rather to provide a realistic conductive path whose thermal influence can be quantified. The objective here is to highlight the significant impact that any conductive interconnection element has on the thermal response of the cells. The extension of the analysis to module-level busbars with more representative geometries is presented in Section 4.4, demonstrating that the proposed modeling approach remains valid irrespective of the specific bar dimensions or layout.

A grid independence study will be first made in order to evaluate the optimal mesh for the computational domain. Then, the results are presented in terms of the maximum and average surface temperatures
T max
,
T avg
, under the various discharge conditions studied, and of the surface temperature field T under a 5C discharge. A comparison will be made between the experimental results and the models results both with and without the inclusion of bars. Subsequently, the results are evaluated using an equivalent boundary condition applied to the tabs, which accounts for the influence of the experimental bars without explicitly including them in the computational domain. Finally, the temperature field of a battery module composed of five cells connected in series is analyzed, with an evaluation of the maximum and average temperatures for the cases where the busbars are either included or omitted from the computational domain. The simulations are performed with COMSOL Multiphysics 6.0, with a time-dependent configuration and the PARDISO direct-sparse solver, using the implicit backward differentiation formula scheme.

In order to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the numerical simulations, a grid independence study was carried out by monitoring the temperature at the center of the battery core
T
c , center
at the end of a 5C discharge for the battery with bars configuration. The 5C discharge condition was chosen for this study because it represents the most demanding scenario in terms of heat generation
Q ̇
and thermal gradients ∇ T within the cell. At higher elementary cell currents
I EC
, the resistive Joule heat generation
Q ̇
co , jou ,
R EC
is maximized, leading to steeper gradients and higher peak temperatures T , which makes the solution more sensitive to discretization errors. By verifying mesh independence under the most critical operating condition, confidence in the adequacy of the selected mesh can be extended to less severe scenarios, such as lower discharge rates (3C). The mesh was progressively refined, starting from a coarse grid with 11,561 elements and increasing to finer grids with up to 237,540 elements. For each mesh, the simulation was executed to the end of the discharge during the experimental setup ( DoD = 57 % ) , and the temperature at the core center was recorded at the latest instant of time t . As shown in Fig. 14 , the computed temperature initially exhibited a noticeable decrease when moving from the coarsest mesh to an intermediate one (from 46.10 °C at 11,561 elements to 45.20 °C at 27,707 elements), indicating that the initial mesh was too coarse to resolve the thermal gradients accurately. Further refinements beyond approximately yielded only marginal changes in the predicted temperature, with differences below 0.1 °C between successive meshes. This indicates that the numerical solution becomes independent of the mesh beyond this resolution. To quantitatively define an acceptable level of error, a maximum temperature error difference
T err
of 0.01 °C was adopted as the convergence criterion. This value was chosen as a reasonable compromise between numerical accuracy and computational cost. Based on this criterion, the mesh with 150,652 elements was deemed sufficiently fine to capture the relevant thermal phenomena while avoiding excessive computational burden. The chosen mesh ensured that the maximum temperature deviation from the finest mesh (237,540 elements) remained within the prescribed tolerance, thereby confirming the reliability of the results. Adopting a mesh that is too coarse can lead to a significant overestimation or underestimation of the peak temperatures and incorrect assessment of the thermal runaway risk. For the configuration without the copper bars, the same validated mesh resolution was employed, with the copper bar components removed from the model. This approach ensured a consistent spatial discretization between configurations, allowing direct comparison of the thermal behavior with and without the bars, while preserving the accuracy achieved in the mesh independence study.

The trends of the maximum and average temperatures,
T avg
,
T max
are computed for both study cases: the first including the bars and the other without bars, for 3C and 5C discharges. For a 5C discharge, the model without considering the bars deviates significantly more from the experimental data compared to the model that includes the bars for both the maximum and average temperatures,
T avg
,
T max
, as illustrated in Fig. 16
. In general, the trend of the model without bars exhibits a more linear behavior, whereas the trend with bars shows a nearly logarithmic pattern. The temperature difference between the cases can primarily be attributed to the absence of contribution of the bars to heat dissipation into the ambient due to their larger heat transfer surface area
A
bar , j
compared to the tabs, whereas they behave as an extended surface. These results demonstrate the necessity of considering the bars in the predictive model, as they behave as heat conductors that help to reduce the battery temperature T . As a consequence, if one doesn't consider the bars, the model fails to predict accurately the experimental battery temperature T . Notably, the average temperature
T avg
aligns more closely with the experimental data than the maximum temperature
T max
. This discrepancy is likely due to numerical artifacts in calculating potential gradients ∇
φ j
, leading to localized hotspots, as well as measurement inaccuracies from the thermal camera.

In Fig. 15, the temperature field T of the battery at the end of a 5C discharge, reaching a DoD of 57 %, is shown for both the experimental analysis results and the model with bars. It is worth noting that the temperature fields are reversed compared to the previous figures, with the axis orientation inverted relative to the y-axis. The temperature fields exhibit similar trends, with a peak temperature difference of 2 °C, within more or less the thermal camera uncertainty band. Additionally, it is observed that the positive tab has higher temperatures than the negative one. This occurs because the positive tab is made of aluminium, which has lower electrical conductivity
σ
t , p
, resulting in higher Joule heat generation
Q ̇
t , p , jou
. This localized heat generation leads to hotspots concentrated in the area between the positive pole and the battery. In addition, the model not only accurately captures the average and maximum temperature trends
T avg
,
T max
but also effectively describes the spatial distribution of temperature T , making it a valuable method for future safety and risk analyses, particularly in the study of localized hotspots. In contrast, the model without bars fails to correctly represent the experimental temperature because it does not account for the heat flux between the tabs and the bars, and the heat losses from the bars in the finite element model. Consequently, the absence of these heat transfer mechanisms leads to less heat dissipation than observed in reality. Since the temperature range of the model without bars deviates significantly from the experimental results, a temperature plot is not shown, as it is not representative of reality.

The temperature T trend during a 3C discharge is illustrated in Fig. 17 . In this scenario, the temperature limit of 50 °C is not reached due to lower heat generation rate, resulting from the reduced elementary cell current
I EC
. Compared to the 5C case, the discrepancy between the model results with and without bars in terms of maximum and average temperatures
T max
,
T avg
is smaller, as the heat flux between the tabs and the bars is reduced. This because generally there is less amount of heat generated so temperatures become lower.

The temperature trends can be divided into three distinct zones. At low DoD (0.00–0.30), the temperature rises rapidly because the heat generation of each component
Q ̇
i
prevails compared to the heat dissipated to the ambient, with the battery temperature T being close to the ambient one
T amb
. However, the slope at the very beginning for the maximum temperature is not that small especially for the model with no bars because of the deviation from the initial uniform temperature condition. At intermediate DoD (0.30–0.60), an inflection point is observed as the heat dissipation to the environment becomes more significant relative to the total heat generation
Q ̇
i
. This occurs because heat generation decreases due to the rise in temperature T , which lowers the internal resistance
R I
, and, consequently, reduces the resistive joule heat generation
Q ̇
co , jou ,
R EC
. In addition, the reversible heat generation
Q ̇
co , rev
is negative because the entropy coefficient ∂
V OC
/ ∂ T is positive is this DoD range. Consequently, during discharge, the negative heat reversible generation
Q ̇
co , rev
reduces the total heat generation inside the core
Q ̇
co
, reducing the temperature slope ∂ T / ∂ t over time t . This effect is not observed in the 5C discharge case because resistive Joule heat generation
Q ̇
co , jou ,
R EC
dominates over reversible heat generation
Q ̇
co , rev
. This is due from the higher elementary cell current
I EC
, as resistive Joule heat generation
Q ̇
co , jou ,
R EC
scales with the square of the elementary cell current
I EC
2
, while reversible heat generation exhibits linear proportionality. Finally, at high DoD (0.60–0.94), the temperature T increases sharply again due to a substantial rise in the resistive Joule heat generation
Q ̇
co , jou ,
R EC
within the battery, because the activation polarization resistance
R
P , ACT
and the concentration polarization resistance
R
P , ACT
result higher at lower SoC levels. The temperature difference between the model with bars and the experimental results is most pronounced at high DoD. This discrepancy is likely attributable to the predicted battery resistance
R B
trend at high DoD, which is derived from the experimental data. Specifically, at high DoD, the activation polarization resistance
R
P , ACT
and the concentration polarization resistance
R
P , ACT
in Eqs. (35)–(36) may deviate slightly from the actual resistance values, leading to the observed differences. This would mean that more data to improve the resistance function to be used in the model, are required.

The higher accuracy of the model with bars is also supported by the root mean square error (RMSE) evaluation of the maximum and average surface temperature,
T max
and
T avg
, for both 5C and 3C discharges, related to the experimental results, reported in Table 7 . The sample points used for a 5C discharge are 24, while for a 3C discharge, 94 sample points are used due to the longer simulation time. It can be observed that all temperature values in the model with bars are closer to those in the model without bars, with almost all cases falling within the measurement accuracy range of the thermal camera. The only exception is the maximum temperature
T max
at some DoDs in the 5C case and at higher DoDs in the 3C case. Nonetheless, shows significantly improved agreement compared to the model without bars, and is still very close to the uncertainty range of the thermal camera. Considering the experimental and modeling uncertainties — including errors in the resistance measurement, open-circuit voltage, and entropy coefficient; uncertainties due to the assumption of constant thermal properties; and approximations introduced by the empirical correlations for natural convection (which approach reduce a lot the computational time compared to an air natural convection simulation) — the model that explicitly accounts for the connecting bars reproduces the experimental for the analyzed conditions. By contrast, the model that omits the bars fails to capture the observed temperature distributions.

The model that includes the bars, and the one with the equivalent boundary condition for tabs via a lumped capacitance model, has been compared through the evaluation of both the maximum and average surface temperature
T max
,
T avg
of the battery, under the same 3C and 5C discharge conditions. A check on the Biot number Bi , was carried out, to verify if the condition Bi ≤ 0.10 is satisfied, as explained in Section 2.3 about the equivalent boundary condition used for tabs. The highest value, Bi = 1.07 ×
10
− 5
is observed on the bar mounted on the positive tab, remaining well below the typical threshold for the lumped capacitance method. This occurs because the positive tab reaches an higher convective heat transfer coefficient
h bar
, because of the higher temperatures due to increased Joule heat generation
Q ̇
t , p , jou
, as discussed in Section 4.1. As shown in Figs. 18–19
, the temperature trends are very similar, with minimal differences that can be attributed to the spatial simplifications introduced by the lumped capacitance method. For the 5C discharge, the maximum temperature difference is 0.46 °C for the
T max
, and 0.18 °C for the
T avg
, while, for the 3C discharge, these differences are 0.57 °C and 0.71 °C, respectively. Hence, they do not significantly affect the overall results, so it is possible to adopt this equivalent boundary condition to reduce the computational domain and simplify the geometry.

Finally, the temperature field T for both the bar model and the lumped capacitance method model are presented in Fig. 20 under the same conditions as the earlier comparison with the experimental model (5C discharge, DoD = 57 % ). The results show that the temperature fields are indeed similar, thereby validating the lumped capacitance method for temperature field analysis. Given its simplicity, faster computation, and the exclusion of additional computational domains, this model is preferable for thermal analysis.

The results are analyzed with reference to the battery module shown in Fig. 11 and described in Section 2.4. A 5C discharge was carried out up to a DoD = 57 % , consistent with the experimental campaign. As shown in Fig. 21 , the trends obtained with the explicit busbar model and with the lumped capacitance approach are very similar, with a maximum deviation of 0.85 °C for
T max
and 0.39 °C for
T avg
, calculated over all the cells in the module. Fig. 22 reports the temperature field at DoD = 57 % , again highlighting that the distributions are almost identical, with hotspot formation close to the positive tab in both cases. Although an optimized condition for natural convection heat transfer was adopted, as explained in Eq. (38), the experimental bars used had a very large exchange surface
A bar
relative to their volume
Λ bar
, effectively reducing the battery temperature. In the present configuration, however, the busbars worsen the thermal situation.

This finding is particularly relevant because it demonstrates that busbars can be removed from the computational domain. In fact, they often either represent very small features, which can deteriorate mesh quality, or large single blocks. In both cases, their omission is justified if the Biot number Bi is below 0.1. As already discussed in Section 2.3, the high thermal conductivity of busbars
k bb
facilitates such low Bi values. Therefore, employing equivalent boundary conditions is a powerful strategy to reduce the computational domain of battery modules and packs without compromising accuracy.

A coupled electro-thermal model of a pouch cell battery is proposed and validated through experimental analysis. This study aims to incorporate the thermal effects of bars mounted on battery tabs, which function as extended surfaces and efficient thermal conductors due to their low thermal resistance. The electrical model is it is two-dimensional, while the thermal model is three-dimensional, solved through a finite element analysis approach with Robin thermal boundary conditions based on literature correlations for air in natural convection.

Three different configurations were developed: one excluding the experimental setup's connection bars, one including them, and one incorporating their thermal effects without explicitly modeling them in the computational domain, using an equivalent boundary condition. The latter approach is justified by the low Biot number, with its maximum value equal to Bi = 1.07 ×
10
− 5
for the bar mounted to the positive tab, allowing their treatment through a lumped capacitance method without resolving their spatial dimensions.

The analyzed battery is an EIG ePLB C020, for which fittings of the open circuit potential
V OC
, resistive parameters R , and entropy coefficient ∂
V OC
/ ∂ T were derived from experimental data from the literature. To accurately capture the entropy coefficient trend, a piecewise fitting approach was employed using three distinct equations based on the SoC range.

Two constant current discharge tests were conducted in the experimental apparatus, 3C and 5C. Maximum and average temperature values,
T max
,
T avg
, as well as the temperature field T , were evaluated using a thermal camera. For a 5C discharge, the model without bars predicts significantly higher temperatures T than the experimental data, with a RMSE of 8.08 °C for the maximum temperature,
T max
and of 2.60 °C for the average temperature
T avg
. In contrast, the model that includes the bars yields much more accurate results, with RMSE values of 2.16 °C for the maximum temperature
T max
, and 0.30 °C for the average temperature
T avg
. Then, a comparison between the model and experimental results considering bars, shows similar temperature distributions T , demonstrating the model's ability to accurately predict the overall temperature field T . For the 3C discharge, the average and maximum temperatures
T max
,
T avg
, show lower RMSE compared to the 5C discharge. For the model including bars, the RMSE is 3.03 °C for the maximum temperature
T max
, and 1.72 °C for the average temperature
T avg
, while for the model without bars, the RMSE values are 1.84 °C and 1.42 °C, respectively. A comparison was conducted between the model that includes the bars and the model that omits them from the computational domain, instead applying a lumped capacitance method with an equivalent boundary condition at the tab's surface. The trends of both maximum and average temperatures
T max
,
T avg
, are very similar for both discharge cases. For the 5C discharge, the maximum temperature variations amount to 0.46 °C for the
T max
, and 0.18 °C for the
T avg
, whereas for the 3C discharge, they reach 0.57 °C and 0.71 °C, respectively. Additionally, the temperature fields T , show a strong similarity, accurately depicting the hotspot that forms near the positive tab.

The methodology was further extended to a battery module consisting of multiple pouch cells. and results demonstrated that the lumped capacitance approach yields trends of
T max
and
T avg
almost identical to those of the explicit busbar model, with maximum deviations of 0.85 °C and 0.39 °C, respectively. This outcome highlights that busbars can be effectively removed from the computational domain and replaced with equivalent boundary conditions, avoiding meshing issues from very small or very large busbar geometries.

Overall, the study demonstrates that the proposed modeling framework accurately predicts the thermal behavior of pouch cells and modules while reducing computational complexity. The use of equivalent boundary conditions for highly conductive auxiliary components such as busbars represents a powerful tool for multi-scale battery thermal management, enabling more efficient and scalable simulations without compromising accuracy.
